{"description":"## Description
Simplify `aimgr clean` so it unconditionally removes everything inside aimgr-owned commands, skills, and agents directories for detected tools.

## Instructions
1. Remove the preview/confirmation flow and delete the `--yes` flag.
2. Reuse the same owned-directory detection model as `repair` and `verify`; extract or reuse a shared helper so all three commands operate on the same owned directories and cleanup boundaries stay consistent.
3. Delete all contents inside owned resource directories, including repo symlinks, broken symlinks, wrong-repo symlinks, regular files, nested namespace content, and stray subdirectories, while leaving the owned root directories themselves in place.
4. Leave non-resource tool config files alone and do not modify `ai.package.yaml`.
5. If no tool directories are detected, exit successfully with an informative no-op message.
6. If `ai.package.yaml` is missing, emit a non-blocking warning that `aimgr repair` will not be able to restore resources.
7. Add structured output for clean so table/text and JSON output can report removed entries and counts for scripting and review.
8. Update command help and examples to describe unconditional cleanup.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/clean.go` — remove interactive behavior, add structured output, and implement full directory-content cleanup
- `cmd/clean_test.go` — cover unconditional wipe semantics, no-op/warning cases, structured output, and untouched manifest/config behavior
- Any shared cleanup helpers needed by the new implementation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr clean` removes every entry inside owned commands/skills/agents directories and leaves those root directories empty
- [ ] `aimgr clean` no longer prompts and no longer exposes `--yes`
- [ ] `ai.package.yaml` remains unchanged after `clean`
- [ ] Non-resource tool files outside owned resource directories remain untouched
- [ ] `aimgr clean` is a no-op with informative output when no tool directories are detected
- [ ] Table/text and JSON output report removed entries with enough detail for scripting/review and report counts consistently
"}

- Remove CLI handling and help text for `--reset` and `--force`
- Reuse the same owned-directory detection model as `clean` and `verify`

## Instructions
1. Reuse or extract shared logic for determining owned resource directories so `repair`, `clean`, and `verify` operate on the same set of folders.
2. Load and validate `ai.package.yaml` before destructive changes. If the manifest is missing or invalid, return an error and make no filesystem changes.
3. Expand package references into the concrete desired resources before reconciling filesystem state.
4. Compute planned installs, fixes/replacements, removals, and optional manifest-prune actions. `--dry-run` must report these planned actions in both table and JSON output without applying them.
5. For each declared resource path, replace conflicting/broken/wrong entries as needed and install or fix resources so all declared resources are healthy.
6. After declared resources are healthy, remove remaining undeclared files, symlinks, and directories from owned resource directories.
7. Preserve/adapt `--prune-package` as a separate manifest-cleanup phase that still validates manifest refs against the repo and works correctly with `--dry-run` and the new output model.
8. Update structured repair output so removals, installs/fixes, prune-package edits, and failures are clearly reported. If the JSON schema changes, treat it as a documented breaking change and coordinate with docs/tests.
9. Update tests for reconcile behavior, dry-run, package expansion, conflicting paths, output, and any repair-related expectations that would otherwise break before the verify task lands.

{"id":"ai-config-manager-thsk.7","title":"Migrate Go module path and install docs to v4","description":"## Description
Major release v4.0.0 requires the Go module path to move from `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v3` to `.../v4`. Without this migration, `go install github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v4/cmd/aimgr@v4.0.0` will fail even if the GitHub release succeeds.

## Instructions
1. Update `go.mod` module path from `/v3` to `/v4`.
2. Update all internal Go imports that reference `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v3/...`.
3. Update release/build metadata injection paths in `.goreleaser.yaml` and `.github/workflows/build.yml` from `/v3/pkg/version` to `/v4/pkg/version`.
4. Update user-facing install documentation from `/v3` to `/v4`, including the README, SECURITY.md, and ai-resource-manager skill docs that show `go install` commands.
5. Run `go mod tidy` after the import/module updates.
6. Do not tag or release as v4.0.0 until this migration is complete.

## Files to Modify
- go.mod (update module path)
- cmd/**/*.go (update internal imports)
- pkg/**/*.go (update internal imports)
- test/**/*.go (update internal imports)
- .goreleaser.yaml (update ldflags import path)
- .github/workflows/build.yml (update ldflags import path)
- README.md (update `go install` path)
- SECURITY.md (update `go install` path)
- ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/SKILL.md (update `go install` path)
- ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/references/install-uninstall.md (update `go install` path)
- ai-resources/skills/ai-resource-manager/references/manage-repository.md (update `go install` path)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `go.mod` declares `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v4`
- [ ] Internal imports no longer reference `/v3`
- [ ] Release/build configs reference `/v4/pkg/version`
- [ ] User-facing install docs use `github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/v4/cmd/aimgr`
- [ ] `go mod tidy` completes successfully
- [ ] The repository builds and tests successfully in the later quality-gate task
","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"hans.kohlreiter@dynatrace.com","created_at":"2026-03-15T20:43:37Z","created_by":"Hans Kohlreiter","updated_at":"2026-03-15T20:43:37Z"}

The v3.4.0 release quality-gates task is blocked by a failing GitHub Actions Build workflow on `main`. Local release checks passed (`make fmt`, `make test`, `make build`), but the latest CI run failed in `golangci-lint`, so release prep must stop until the branch is green or the failure is explicitly understood.

## Context
- Workflow: Build
- Run ID: `23116439511`
- URL: https://github.com/dynatrace-oss/ai-config-manager/actions/runs/23116439511
- Failed job: `Test`
- Failed step: `golangci-lint`

## Lint Errors
1. `cmd/project_verify.go:736:91`: `fixVerifyIssues - result 0 (error) is always nil (unparam)`
2. `cmd/repair.go:358:26`: `desiredInstallPaths - projectPath is unused (unparam)`

## Instructions
1. Reproduce the lint failure locally with the same linter class used in CI.
2. Fix the two `unparam` findings in `cmd/project_verify.go` and `cmd/repair.go`.
3. Re-run the relevant local quality checks.
4. Re-run or verify CI is green after the fix.
5. Do not continue the v3.4.0 release until this blocker is resolved or explicitly waived.

## Files to Modify
- cmd/project_verify.go
- cmd/repair.go
- Related tests if needed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The two reported `unparam` issues are fixed
- [ ] Local lint/quality checks pass
- [ ] GitHub Actions Build workflow no longer fails for this reason
- [ ] Release task ai-config-manager-l8mc.3 can proceed without unresolved CI blocker","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"hans.kohlreiter@dynatrace.com","created_at":"2026-03-16T21:09:51Z","created_by":"Hans Kohlreiter","updated_at":"2026-03-16T21:09:51Z"}

{"description":"## Description
Create the release preparation commit after the changelog updates and the CI lint-fix changes are ready, then push that commit to origin/main so the tag is based on a clean remote state.

## Instructions
1. Stage the release documentation changes, the CI lint-fix changes, and the bd tracker state exported by this release workflow.
2. Create a conventional commit with message `release: prepare v3.4.0`.
3. Run `git pull --rebase`.
4. Run `bd sync`.
5. Push the commit to `origin/main`.
6. Verify the branch is up to date with origin before creating the tag.
7. Wait for the Build workflow on the pushed commit to complete successfully before continuing to tagging.

## Files to Modify
- CHANGELOG.md (already updated by earlier task)
- cmd/project_verify.go (lint-fix cleanup)
- cmd/project_verify_test.go (align tests with lint-fix cleanup)
- cmd/repair.go (lint-fix cleanup)
- cmd/repair_test.go (align tests with lint-fix cleanup)
- .beads/backup/backup_state.json (auto-updated by bd)
- .beads/backup/dependencies.jsonl (auto-updated by bd)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Release prep commit exists with the changelog updates and CI lint-fix changes for v3.4.0
- [ ] `git pull --rebase` succeeds before push
- [ ] `bd sync` completes successfully
- [ ] The commit is pushed to origin/main
- [ ] The branch reports up to date with `origin/main`
- [ ] The Build workflow on the pushed commit succeeds before tagging
"}

{"description":"## Description
Batch ai.package.yaml updates after an install run. The current `install` command iterates over successful and skipped results and calls a helper that loads or creates the manifest, adds one resource, and saves immediately. Installing multiple resources therefore performs repeated manifest reads and writes inside a single invocation.

## Instructions
1. Update `cmd/install.go` so the install results path first collects every successful or skipped resource reference that should be persisted.
2. Replace the per-resource `updateManifest(projectPath, resourceRef)` loop with a batched helper that:
   - returns immediately when `installNoSaveFlag`, `!installSaveFlag`, or `installingFromManifest` is true
   - loads or creates `ai.package.yaml` exactly once
   - adds each eligible resource via `m.Add(...)` so manifest-level deduplication remains unchanged
   - saves the manifest once after all additions are processed
3. Preserve current install semantics:
   - failed resources are not added to the manifest
   - skipped/already-installed resources remain eligible to be recorded
   - manifest update failures still surface as warnings without hiding install failures
4. Add or update focused tests in `cmd/install_test.go` that cover a multi-resource install/save path and verify the final manifest contents.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/install.go` — batch manifest updates instead of mutating the manifest once per result
- `cmd/install_test.go` — add or adjust coverage for multi-resource manifest persistence

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The install results loop no longer loads and saves the manifest once per resource
- [ ] A single install invocation writes `ai.package.yaml` at most once when saving is enabled
- [ ] Failed resources are excluded and skipped/already-installed resources remain eligible for manifest persistence
- [ ] Existing install behavior stays compatible aside from eliminating redundant manifest writes
- [ ] `go test ./cmd -run Test.*Install` passes
"}

{"description":"## Description
Cache the global configuration used by installer symlink-source resolution. `Installer.getSymlinkSource()` currently calls `config.LoadGlobal()` on every invocation even though a single installer instance may resolve multiple resources across multiple tools within one command.

## Instructions
1. Extend `pkg/install/installer.go` so an `Installer` instance keeps cached global-config state for symlink source resolution.
2. Replace the direct `config.LoadGlobal()` call inside `getSymlinkSource()` with a helper that lazy-loads the config once per installer instance and reuses the cached mappings on later calls.
3. Preserve current fallback behavior: if the global config cannot be loaded, the installer should continue to use the original resource path instead of failing the install.
4. Keep the existing modification lookup behavior for commands, skills, and agents: when a tool-specific modification path exists it should still be preferred over the original resource path.
5. Add or update tests in `pkg/install/installer_test.go` to cover both modified-path selection and fallback-to-original behavior with the cached implementation.

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/install/installer.go` — cache global config used by symlink-source resolution
- `pkg/install/installer_test.go` — add or adjust coverage for cached config behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `getSymlinkSource()` no longer calls `config.LoadGlobal()` on every invocation
- [ ] A single `Installer` instance loads global config at most once before reusing cached state
- [ ] Tool-specific modification paths are still preferred when available
- [ ] Config-load failures still fall back to the original resource path without failing installation
- [ ] `go test ./pkg/install` passes
"}

{"description":"## Description
Reuse tool detection and installer construction during `project verify --fix`. `fixVerifyIssues()` currently detects tools and builds a new installer separately inside each fixable issue branch, so a run with multiple `broken`, `wrong-repo`, or `not-installed` issues repeats the same setup work.

## Instructions
1. Update `cmd/project_verify.go` so `fixVerifyIssues()` has a shared, lazily initialized installer setup for fixable issues.
2. Detect existing tools and construct `install.NewInstallerWithTargets(projectPath, detectedTools)` once per `fixVerifyIssues()` invocation, only when the first reinstall/install action actually needs it.
3. Reuse the cached installer for both the `broken`/`wrong-repo` path and the `not-installed` path.
4. Preserve current behavior for repo existence checks, per-issue error handling, and user-facing messages.
5. Add or update tests in `cmd/project_verify_test.go` to cover multiple fixable issues in one run or otherwise verify that broken, wrong-repo, and not-installed resources still behave correctly with the shared installer setup.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/project_verify.go` — reuse detected tools and installer construction within `fixVerifyIssues()`
- `cmd/project_verify_test.go` — add or adjust coverage for the shared installer path

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `fixVerifyIssues()` no longer repeats `tools.DetectExistingTools()` and `install.NewInstallerWithTargets()` inside each fixable issue branch
- [ ] Broken and wrong-repo resources are still reinstalled from the active repo
- [ ] Not-installed resources are still installed when they exist in the repo
- [ ] Resources missing from the repo continue to report the current recovery guidance without unnecessary installer setup
- [ ] `go test ./cmd -run TestFixVerifyIssues` passes
"}

{"description":"## Description
Consolidate the `aimgr list` optimization work that was previously split across two overlapping tickets. The current list command constructs repo managers in multiple helpers and recomputes the expanded manifest for table output even though all of that work belongs to a single command invocation.

## Instructions
1. Update `cmd/list_installed.go` so `aimgr list` creates the repo manager, when available, at most once per command invocation and passes it to helpers that need package metadata.
2. Compute the expanded manifest once per command invocation and reuse it for resource info construction and table output instead of recomputing it inside `outputInstalledTable()`.
3. Remove the duplicate `NewManagerWithLogLevel()` usage from `appendManifestPackages()`, `expandManifestResources()`, and `outputInstalledTable()` by introducing shared per-invocation state or equivalent helper parameters.
4. Preserve graceful degradation: if the repo manager cannot be created, the manifest is missing, or a package cannot be loaded, the list command should continue to return output without failing.
5. Add or update tests in `cmd/list_installed_test.go` so sync-status and package-display paths continue to behave correctly with the shared manager/manifest state.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/list_installed.go` — reuse repo-manager and expanded-manifest state across the list pipeline
- `cmd/list_installed_test.go` — add or adjust coverage for the shared list state

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `aimgr list` no longer constructs separate repo managers inside `appendManifestPackages()`, `expandManifestResources()`, and `outputInstalledTable()` during one invocation
- [ ] Table output reuses a precomputed expanded manifest instead of recalculating it inside `outputInstalledTable()`
- [ ] Package descriptions, package resource counts, and sync symbols remain unchanged
- [ ] The command still tolerates missing manifest, repo, and package data without failing
- [ ] `go test ./cmd -run 'TestListInstalled|TestBuildResourceInfo'` passes
"}

Consolidate the `aimgr list` optimization work that was previously split across two overlapping tickets. The current list command constructs repo managers in multiple helpers and recomputes the expanded manifest for table output even though all of that work belongs to a single command invocation.

## Instructions
1. Update `cmd/list_installed.go` so `aimgr list` creates the repo manager, when available, at most once per command invocation.
2. Thread the shared list state through the full command pipeline as needed (`RunE` → `appendManifestPackages` → `buildResourceInfo` / manifest expansion → `outputInstalledTable`) so package metadata and manifest expansion are reused instead of recomputed.
3. Compute the expanded manifest once per command invocation and reuse it for resource info construction and table output instead of recomputing it inside `outputInstalledTable()`.
4. Remove the duplicate `NewManagerWithLogLevel()` usage from `appendManifestPackages()`, `expandManifestResources()`, and `outputInstalledTable()` by introducing shared per-invocation state or equivalent helper parameters.
5. Preserve graceful degradation: if the repo manager cannot be created, the manifest is missing, or a package cannot be loaded, the list command should continue to return output without failing.
6. Add or update tests in `cmd/list_installed_test.go` so sync-status and package-display paths continue to behave correctly with the shared manager/manifest state.

{"id":"ai-config-manager-t4s","title":"Batch manifest load/save in install command instead of per-resource","description":"## Description
Batch ai.package.yaml updates after an install run. The current `install` command iterates over successful and skipped results and calls a helper that loads or creates the manifest, adds one resource, and saves immediately. Installing multiple resources therefore performs repeated manifest reads and writes inside a single invocation.

## Instructions
1. Update `cmd/install.go` so the install results path first collects every successful or skipped resource reference that should be persisted.
2. Replace the per-resource `updateManifest(projectPath, resourceRef)` loop with a batched helper that:
   - returns immediately when `installNoSaveFlag`, `!installSaveFlag`, or `installingFromManifest` is true
   - loads or creates `ai.package.yaml` exactly once
   - adds each eligible resource via `m.Add(...)` so manifest-level deduplication remains unchanged
   - saves the manifest once after all additions are processed
3. Preserve current install semantics:
   - failed resources are not added to the manifest
   - skipped/already-installed resources remain eligible to be recorded
   - manifest update failures still surface as warnings without hiding install failures
4. Add or update focused tests in `cmd/install_test.go` that cover a multi-resource install/save path and verify the final manifest contents.

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/install.go` — batch manifest updates instead of mutating the manifest once per result
- `cmd/install_test.go` — add or adjust coverage for multi-resource manifest persistence

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The install results loop no longer loads and saves the manifest once per resource
- [ ] A single install invocation writes `ai.package.yaml` at most once when saving is enabled
- [ ] Failed resources are excluded and skipped/already-installed resources remain eligible for manifest persistence
- [ ] Existing install behavior stays compatible aside from eliminating redundant manifest writes
- [ ] `go test ./cmd -run Test.*Install` passes

{"id":"ai-config-manager-7cb","title":"Cache config.LoadGlobal() result in Installer.getSymlinkSource","description":"## Description
Cache the global configuration used by installer symlink-source resolution. `Installer.getSymlinkSource()` currently calls `config.LoadGlobal()` on every invocation even though a single installer instance may resolve multiple resources across multiple tools within one command.

## Instructions
1. Extend `pkg/install/installer.go` so an `Installer` instance keeps cached global-config state for symlink source resolution.
2. Replace the direct `config.LoadGlobal()` call inside `getSymlinkSource()` with a helper that lazy-loads the config once per installer instance and reuses the cached mappings on later calls.
3. Preserve current fallback behavior: if the global config cannot be loaded, the installer should continue to use the original resource path instead of failing the install.
4. Keep the existing modification lookup behavior for commands, skills, and agents: when a tool-specific modification path exists it should still be preferred over the original resource path.
5. Add or update tests in `pkg/install/installer_test.go` to cover both modified-path selection and fallback-to-original behavior with the cached implementation.

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/install/installer.go` — cache global config used by symlink-source resolution
- `pkg/install/installer_test.go` — add or adjust coverage for cached config behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `getSymlinkSource()` no longer calls `config.LoadGlobal()` on every invocation
- [ ] A single `Installer` instance loads global config at most once before reusing cached state
- [ ] Tool-specific modification paths are still preferred when available
- [ ] Config-load failures still fall back to the original resource path without failing installation
- [ ] `go test ./pkg/install` passes

{"id":"ai-config-manager-0v6k","title":"Track Codecov embedded actions/github-script Node.js 24 support","description":"## Description
The build workflow migration to Node.js 24-compatible action versions is complete, but the Codecov step still emits a GitHub warning because `codecov/codecov-action@v5` currently downloads and runs `actions/github-script`, which is still on Node.js 20.

## Context
- Verified in Build workflow run `23167318225`
- Remaining warning points to `actions/github-script@60a0d83039c74a4aee543508d2ffcb1c3799cdea`
- The repository workflow already uses `FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24: true`
- Direct workflow-owned action upgrades are already done in `.github/workflows/build.yml` and `.github/workflows/release.yml`

## Instructions
1. Check whether a newer `codecov/codecov-action` release removes the embedded `actions/github-script` Node.js 20 warning.
2. If an upstream fix exists, update `.github/workflows/build.yml` to the safe version and verify the Build workflow again.
3. If no upstream fix exists, capture the rationale and any upstream tracking link so the warning remains explicitly understood.

## Files to Modify
- .github/workflows/build.yml (only if a newer safe Codecov action exists)
- Related docs only if local documentation is needed for an intentional hold

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Remaining Codecov-related Node.js 20 warning is either removed or explicitly documented with rationale
- [ ] Any workflow change is validated with a successful Build run
- [ ] The follow-up clearly records whether the remaining warning is blocked on upstream action maintenance
","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","owner":"hans.kohlreiter@dynatrace.com","created_at":"2026-03-17T08:06:19Z","created_by":"Hans Kohlreiter","updated_at":"2026-03-17T08:06:19Z"}

{"description":"## Description
User decision: remove Codecov integration instead of waiting for an upstream Node.js 24 fix in `codecov/codecov-action`.

## Context
- The remaining Node.js 20 deprecation warning comes from Codecov's embedded `actions/github-script@v7` dependency, not from workflow-owned actions in this repository.
- This is a Go project; coverage generation can remain local/GitHub-native without an external Codecov dependency.
- Current Codecov usage is non-blocking (`fail_ci_if_error: false`) and mainly provides an external badge and coverage UI.

## Instructions
1. Remove the Codecov upload step from `.github/workflows/build.yml`.
2. Remove repository files/configuration that only support Codecov.
3. Remove README references that advertise Codecov coverage.
4. Keep CI green after the cleanup.

## Files to Modify
- .github/workflows/build.yml
- README.md
- codecov.yml (remove if no longer used)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Build workflow no longer uses `codecov/codecov-action`
- [ ] Repository no longer contains unused Codecov-only configuration
- [ ] README no longer links to or displays a Codecov badge
- [ ] Relevant quality checks pass after the removal
"}
